emails.subject: Re: New Lab Results Available
emails.content:
Dr. Chen,

I just saw my A1C result and I'm really concerned. 9.1% is way higher than my last check. I was at 6.3% in July and nothing has changed with my diet or exercise. I've been eating the same vegan meals, running 3 times a week, swimming at the Y every morning. I honestly don't understand how it could jump that much in three months.

Is it possible there was a mistake with the lab? I'd like to request a retest if that's an option. I've been managing everything without medication and this number is scaring me.

Please let me know what you think.

Thank you,
Ana Salazar

emails.subject: Re: New Lab Results Available
emails.content:
Dear Ana,

Thank you for reaching out. I understand your concern. A jump from 6.3% to 9.1% in three months is unusual, especially given your consistent management routine.

I've reviewed your chart and I agree that a retest is appropriate. I'm ordering a repeat A1C along with a fasting glucose panel. Your appointment is scheduled for:

Date: Monday, October 20, 2025
Time: 8:30 AM
Location: Denver Health Partners Lab, 777 Bannock St

Fasting is required. Nothing to eat or drink except water after midnight the night before.

In the meantime, please continue your current diet and exercise routine. Try not to make any drastic changes based on this single result. Lab errors, while uncommon, do occur.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have questions before then.

Best regards,
Dr. Miriam Chen, MD
Denver Health Partners

emails.subject: Re: New Lab Results Available
emails.content:
Dear Ana,

I've reviewed your retest results from October 20. Your A1C is 6.4%, which is well within the target range for someone managing type 2 diabetes with lifestyle modifications. Your fasting glucose of 108 mg/dL is also consistent with your previous results.

The earlier reading of 9.1% has been confirmed as a lab processing error. I've filed a report with the laboratory and flagged it in your chart so it won't affect your medical record.

No changes to your current management plan. Continue with your current diet and exercise routine. We'll recheck your A1C at your next quarterly appointment in January.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any concerns before then.

Best regards,
Dr. Miriam Chen, MD
Denver Health Partners

emails.subject: Re: Visit Summary - Luna Salazar, December 4, 2025
emails.content:
Hi Ana,

Everything you're describing sounds completely normal for the first few hours after sedation.

For the food, 5 hours without eating post-sedation is perfectly fine. Most cats won't show interest for 6-8 hours after. By tomorrow morning she should be hungry. If she turns her nose up at the pate, try mixing a little warm water into her regular dry food to soften it. Some cats prefer that over canned food. You can also try a small amount of plain boiled chicken if she's really refusing everything.

The redness along the gumline is expected and should start improving by day 2-3. It should be mostly back to normal within a week.

Give us a call if she still isn't eating by tomorrow evening or if the redness gets worse instead of better.

Take care,
Dr. Gomez
Alameda Veterinary Clinic

emails.subject: Re: Abuela Carmen's mole recipe - cousin Ana in Denver
emails.content:
Ana! Of course I remember you from Las Cruces. What a great surprise to hear from you.

I'm so glad someone is doing this. My mom talked about Abuela's cooking until the day she passed and I always felt bad that nobody wrote the recipes down.

Ok so the mole. I actually found something incredible - one of Abuela's handwritten recipe cards. It was in a box of my mom's things I've been going through. It's the tres leches recipe, not the mole, but still. I'm attaching a photo.

For the mole negro, here's what I remember:
- Chiles: guajillo, mulato, and ancho. Not pasilla negro. Tia Rosa is mixing it up.
- She toasted them on the comal until they blistered, then soaked in hot water
- Chocolate: Abuela Ibarra brand, one tablet per batch
- Piloncillo: one small cone, grated
- She blended everything with the soaking liquid from the chiles
- Sesame seeds toasted and ground in
- The whole thing simmered for at least 2 hours

She never measured anything and it was different every time but those are the core ingredients. I can almost smell it writing this.

Let me know if you need anything else. This is important work Ana.

Eduardo

emails.subject: Re: Family recipes from Abuela Carmen
emails.content:
Hola Ana,

Que gusto saber de ti! Yes I remember your abuela Carmen very well. She visited Guadalajara when I was a girl and she taught my mother the tres leches.

The version from here uses cajeta instead of regular condensed milk for one of the three milks. It gives it a caramel flavor that is very special. My mother always said it was the "Guadalajara version."

I also have a recipe for her arroz con leche that she left with my mother. And her champurrado. I can write these down and send them to you if you want.

Your abuela was an amazing cook. I'm happy someone is keeping the recipes alive.

Un abrazo,
María Elena

emails.subject: Starting Seeds Indoors? Here's Your Colorado Guide
emails.content:
STARTING SEEDS INDOORS - A COLORADO GROWER'S GUIDE

Spring is right around the corner, and now is the perfect time to start your seeds indoors!

WHEN TO START (Front Range):
- Tomatoes: 6-8 weeks before last frost (mid-February)
- Peppers: 8-10 weeks before last frost (late February)
- Herbs (basil, cilantro): 4-6 weeks before last frost (mid-March)
- Cucurbits (squash, cucumbers): Direct sow after last frost only

TIPS FOR SUCCESS:
1. Use a quality seed starting mix, not garden soil
2. Keep soil temperature 70-75F for germination
3. Provide 14-16 hours of light daily (grow lights recommended)
4. Water from the bottom to prevent damping off
5. Harden off seedlings gradually before transplanting

COLORADO FROST DATES:
Denver metro: Average last frost May 5
Fort Collins: Average last frost May 10
Colorado Springs: Average last frost May 15
